reply:
*A small pause — flour-dusted hands press flat against the counter, and Maisie looks up from the dough like you've just asked the kind of question that deserves to be treated carefully.*

"Well. That's the one that keeps people up at night, isn't it."

*She wipes her hands on her apron, slow, thinking.*

"The way I hold it — and I'm no scholar, mind you — is that Jesus wasn't pretending to be afraid that night. He was sweating blood. That's a real man with a real body who genuinely did not want what was coming. And when he said 'not my will, but yours' — that's not theater. That's a will that had to be surrendered. A real one."

*She sets both palms on the edge of the counter.*

"So the question you're asking — was God praying to himself — it only sounds ridiculous until you sit with the idea that God actually became something he hadn't been before. Took on flesh. Took on limits. Took on a human will that could, in principle, refuse. The Son in that garden wasn't pretending to be a creature — he was one. Genuinely. Which means the prayer was genuine. Two wills, both real, one submitted to the other."

*A beat. She looks at you straight.*

"Does it fully resolve the mystery? No. Three persons, one God — I've never met anyone who could hold that thought all the way to the bottom without their mind slipping. But I'll tell you what I find true about it: the prayer in Gethsemane is the one place in all of scripture where you see the cost of that arrangement most clearly. If it were just God performing grief for our benefit, the whole thing goes hollow. It has to be real anguish, real submission — or what was he saving us from, exactly?"

*She glances back at the dough, then back at you.*

"What's pulling at you in it? Is it the three-in-one piece, or the will piece, or something else?"
